faqs.q: How do solar panels work?
faqs.a: Solar panels convert sunlight into direct current (DC) electricity using photovoltaic cells. An inverter then converts that DC into alternating current (AC) that powers your home. Excess electricity is exported to the grid or stored in a battery.

faqs.q: What is a solar PV system?
faqs.a: PV stands for photovoltaic. A solar PV system is a set of panels, an inverter, mounting hardware, cables and a monitoring system that generates electricity from sunlight for residential or commercial use.

faqs.q: What is the difference between on-grid, off-grid and hybrid solar?
faqs.a: On-grid systems are connected to the electricity grid and don't work in a blackout. Off-grid systems rely on batteries and have no grid connection. Hybrid systems are connected to the grid and include batteries for backup and self-consumption.

faqs.q: Can solar panels generate electricity on cloudy days?
faqs.a: Yes, but at reduced output — typically 10–40% of a clear day, depending on cloud density. Modern panels still produce useful power in overcast conditions and respond quickly to light changes.

faqs.q: Do solar panels work at night?
faqs.a: No. Panels need light to produce electricity. At night your home draws from the battery (if installed) or from the grid.

faqs.q: What is a kilowatt (kW) versus a kilowatt-hour (kWh)?
faqs.a: A kilowatt (kW) is a measure of power — the capacity to generate or use electricity at an instant. A kilowatt-hour (kWh) is a measure of energy — 1 kW sustained for one hour. Your system is rated in kW; your bill is in kWh.

faqs.q: How many solar panels do I need for my home?
faqs.a: A typical Australian home needs 15–25 panels for a 6.6kW–10kW system. Exact count depends on your daily usage, roof space, orientation and budget.

faqs.q: What is a typical Australian residential system size?
faqs.a: Most new residential installs in 2026 are between 6.6kW and 13kW. The 10kW single-phase system is now the most common size in the Illawarra.

faqs.q: Can I add more panels to my existing system later?
faqs.a: Sometimes. It depends on your inverter capacity, switchboard space, and Endeavour/Essential Energy approval. Many homeowners find it cheaper to install a larger system upfront than to expand later.

faqs.q: What is net metering?
faqs.a: Net metering (sometimes called 'net billing' in NSW) credits you for solar energy you export to the grid. The credit rate is called the feed-in tariff and is lower than the retail rate you pay for grid imports.

faqs.q: What is a gross meter vs a net meter?
faqs.a: A gross meter records all solar you generate. A net meter records only what you export after self-consumption. Almost all new NSW installs use net metering via a smart meter.

faqs.q: Do I need a smart meter for solar?
faqs.a: Yes. NSW requires a smart (Type 4 or Type 5) meter for any new solar installation. Your retailer arranges the upgrade; most homeowners pay no upfront cost.

faqs.q: How long do solar panels last?
faqs.a: Quality Tier 1 panels carry a 25–30-year product warranty and a performance warranty guaranteeing at least 85% output at year 25. Real-world lifespan is commonly 30+ years with mild degradation.

faqs.q: What is panel degradation?
faqs.a: Panels lose a small amount of efficiency each year. Tier 1 panels degrade 0.25–0.5% per year — after 25 years they still produce 85–90% of their original output.

faqs.q: Are solar panels environmentally friendly?
faqs.a: Yes. A residential panel typically offsets its manufacturing carbon footprint within 2–3 years and continues to generate clean energy for 25+ years.

faqs.q: Can I go 100% off-grid with solar?
faqs.a: Technically yes, with enough panels and battery capacity. For most suburban homes in NSW it's cheaper and more reliable to stay grid-connected and offset with solar + battery.

faqs.q: What happens to excess solar energy?
faqs.a: Without a battery, excess energy is exported to the grid and earns a feed-in tariff credit. With a battery, excess charges the battery first and then exports once full.

faqs.q: Does solar reduce grid reliance?
faqs.a: Yes. A 6.6kW system typically offsets 50–70% of a home's grid electricity. Adding a battery pushes that to 80–95%.

faqs.q: Can I use solar during a blackout?
faqs.a: Only if your system has a battery with backup capability and the correct switchgear. Standard grid-tied solar shuts down in a blackout for safety reasons.

faqs.q: What is solar irradiance?
faqs.a: Solar irradiance is the amount of solar energy reaching a surface, measured in kWh/m²/day. Wollongong averages 4.6–4.9 kWh/m²/day — well above the UK or Germany.

faqs.q: Why do solar panels point north in Australia?
faqs.a: Because we're in the Southern Hemisphere, a true-north-facing roof receives the most direct sunlight over the year. Panels on east- or west-facing roofs produce 10–20% less annual energy.

faqs.q: Can solar panels overheat?
faqs.a: Panels are less efficient when hot — most lose about 0.3–0.4% efficiency per degree above 25°C. That's why peak summer output in Wollongong isn't much higher than spring despite more sunshine.

faqs.q: What is the 'solar duck curve'?
faqs.a: The duck curve describes grid demand dropping mid-day (when solar is abundant) and spiking in the evening. It's why feed-in tariffs have dropped and batteries have become more valuable.

faqs.q: Do solar panels produce the same power all day?
faqs.a: No. Production follows a bell curve — low in the morning, peak around solar noon, and low again in the afternoon. Cloud cover and shading introduce dips.

faqs.q: Can I run my whole house on solar?
faqs.a: Yes, with a properly sized system and battery. Most homes don't need to go fully self-sufficient to see major bill savings — 70–80% offset is the economic sweet spot.

faqs.q: What size solar system do I need?
faqs.a: As a rule of thumb, divide your daily kWh usage by 4 (Illawarra sun hours) and add 20–30% for efficiency losses. A home using 25 kWh/day typically needs 7–9 kW of panels.

faqs.q: Is a 6.6kW system big enough?
faqs.a: For homes using up to ~20 kWh/day with low evening use, yes. For larger homes, EVs, or homes with heat pumps, 10kW is usually a better fit.

faqs.q: Should I get 6.6kW or 10kW?
faqs.a: In the Illawarra, 10kW is the better choice for most homes in 2026 — about 35–40% more cost for 50% more energy, plus future-proofing for batteries and EVs.

faqs.q: Can a single-phase home have 10kW of solar?
faqs.a: Yes. Endeavour and Essential Energy routinely approve 10kW on single-phase homes, usually with an 8–10kW inverter and export limiting if needed.

faqs.q: How much does a 13kW system cost?
faqs.a: A quality 13kW system in the Illawarra costs around $13,500–$16,500 installed after STC rebate. Most 13kW installs need 3-phase power.

faqs.q: What is oversizing?
faqs.a: Oversizing is installing more panels than the inverter's rated output — e.g., 6.6kW of panels on a 5kW inverter. The CEC permits up to 33% oversizing for better morning/afternoon production.

faqs.q: Can I install an undersized inverter on purpose?
faqs.a: Yes. Up to 133% DC/AC ratio is standard. It boosts output in shoulder hours without affecting the STC rebate.

faqs.q: How much roof space do I need for 6.6kW?
faqs.a: Roughly 33–40 m² of usable roof — around 18–20 panels at 1.7 m² each.

faqs.q: How much roof space does 10kW need?
faqs.a: About 55–65 m² — typically 24–28 panels depending on wattage. Larger homes or those with hip roofs often split across multiple roof faces.

faqs.q: Is it better to oversize my solar to account for future needs?
faqs.a: Usually yes. Adding panels later is almost always more expensive per kW than installing them upfront, and you capture a larger STC rebate now.

faqs.q: How do I know how much electricity my house uses?
faqs.a: Check your power bill for daily or quarterly kWh usage. The 'average daily usage' figure is the most useful. A typical NSW family home uses 18–28 kWh/day.

faqs.q: Should I size my system to my current or future use?
faqs.a: Size for 3–5 years ahead. Most households use more electricity each year as appliances become electrified (aircon, EVs, induction cooktops).

faqs.q: How much does each extra kW produce?
faqs.a: In the Illawarra, each kW of north-facing panels produces about 1,450–1,550 kWh per year.

faqs.q: Will a bigger system save me more money?
faqs.a: Usually yes, up to the point where you're exporting too much cheap energy. Pairing a larger system with a battery is the best way to maximise savings.

faqs.q: Is it possible to install too much solar?
faqs.a: Yes, if export limits or low self-consumption mean a lot of generation is wasted. The sweet spot for most Illawarra homes is 10kW single-phase or 13kW three-phase.

faqs.q: What if my roof is too small for a big system?
faqs.a: You can use higher-wattage panels (420–440W+) to fit more power in less space, or split panels across multiple orientations.

faqs.q: Can panels go on a flat roof?
faqs.a: Yes, using tilt frames. This is common on skillion and flat-roofed homes around Austinmer and Coledale.

faqs.q: Are east/west facing panels worth it?
faqs.a: Yes. They produce 10–20% less annually than north, but spread generation across the day — improving self-consumption for homes with morning and afternoon loads.

faqs.q: Can I split panels between north, east and west?
faqs.a: Absolutely. Many Illawarra roofs use split arrays to maximise usable roof and match household consumption curves.

faqs.q: Should I upsize now or expand later?
faqs.a: Upsize now. Adding panels later often requires a new inverter, re-approval from Endeavour/Essential Energy, and a higher per-kW cost.

faqs.q: How do you calculate payback for different sizes?
faqs.a: Payback = Net cost ÷ (self-consumed kWh × retail rate + exported kWh × feed-in tariff). Larger systems typically have similar payback periods with higher lifetime savings.

faqs.q: Is there a maximum system size allowed in NSW?
faqs.a: For single-phase: usually 10kW inverter / 13.3kW of panels. For three-phase: 30kW inverter / 39.9kW of panels. Anything larger requires special DNSP approval.

faqs.q: What does 'export limiting' mean?
faqs.a: Export limiting is a software setting on your inverter that caps how much power can be sent to the grid. It lets you install a bigger system in constrained grid areas.

faqs.q: Does export limiting reduce my savings?
faqs.a: Only marginally for most homes. You still consume all the solar you use yourself — limits only cap the cheap exports.

faqs.q: Should I include an EV in my sizing calculation?
faqs.a: Yes. An EV adds 10–20 kWh/day of consumption when driven regularly. Most EV-owning households find 10–13kW of solar worthwhile.

faqs.q: How much does a 6.6kW solar system cost in NSW?
faqs.a: After the federal STC rebate, expect $6,800–$8,500 installed for a quality 6.6kW system in the Illawarra in 2026.

faqs.q: How much does a 10kW solar system cost?
faqs.a: $10,800–$13,500 installed in the Illawarra after the STC rebate, depending on panel brand, inverter, and roof complexity.

faqs.q: How much does a 13kW system cost?
faqs.a: $13,500–$16,500 installed after rebate, usually requiring 3-phase power.

faqs.q: Why are some quotes so much cheaper?
faqs.a: Cheap quotes typically use low-tier panels, no-name inverters, non-CEC installers, or cut corners on mounting and cabling. Warranties are often unserviced. If a quote is 30%+ below market, something's missing.

faqs.q: What is included in a 'quality' solar quote?
faqs.a: Tier 1 panels, a reputable inverter (Fronius, Sungrow, SMA, Enphase), full CEC-accredited installation, 10+ year workmanship warranty, monitoring, all approvals and a written performance estimate.

faqs.q: Is $3,000 for a 6.6kW system too cheap?
faqs.a: Yes. At that price the system either uses bottom-tier components, isn't CEC-accredited, or is missing key items (labour, mounting, or DNSP fees). Avoid.

faqs.q: Are there any hidden costs?
faqs.a: Watch for extras like meter upgrades, switchboard upgrades, tile replacement, scaffolding on steep roofs, or additional travel charges. A reputable installer includes all standard costs in the quote.

faqs.q: Do I need to pay a deposit?
faqs.a: Most installers take 10–20% deposit with the contract. Avoid quotes that demand 50%+ upfront or full payment before install.

faqs.q: When is the balance due?
faqs.a: Usually on the day of installation or upon commissioning. Paying after the final sign-off is ideal.

faqs.q: Are prices negotiable?
faqs.a: Some flexibility exists, especially on package deals and if you're buying solar + battery together. But heavy discounts usually mean corners are being cut.

faqs.q: Is solar cheaper if I pay cash vs finance?
faqs.a: Cash is usually marginally cheaper because finance costs are built into some financed deals. Run the maths on effective cost either way.

faqs.q: How much does a battery add to the cost?
faqs.a: A 10kWh battery installed costs $8,500–$10,500 in 2026 after the NSW Cheaper Home Batteries Program rebate.

faqs.q: Is a solar + battery package cheaper than buying separately?
faqs.a: Often by $500–$1,500 due to shared installation labour and bundled component discounts.

faqs.q: Do prices vary by region in NSW?
faqs.a: Yes. Metro Sydney is the cheapest; regional NSW is slightly higher due to travel and fewer installers. The Illawarra sits in between.

faqs.q: Does a steeper roof cost more?
faqs.a: Usually yes. Roofs over 25° or two-storey homes may add $200–$600 for scaffolding and safety gear.

faqs.q: Do tile roofs cost more than tin?
faqs.a: Marginally. Tile installs take slightly longer and include replacement of any broken tiles. Allow $100–$300 extra.

faqs.q: Does my switchboard need upgrading?
faqs.a: Older switchboards (pre-2000) may need upgrading to accommodate solar. A typical upgrade costs $800–$1,800. Your installer will assess this on the site visit.

faqs.q: What's the average cost per watt in NSW?
faqs.a: In 2026, quality installs cost $1.05–$1.30 per watt after rebate. Budget installs run $0.80–$1.00/W but often use inferior components.

faqs.q: Does GST apply to solar?
faqs.a: Yes, GST is charged on the system cost. It's typically already included in the quoted price for residential.

faqs.q: Are there tax deductions for residential solar?
faqs.a: Not for residential homes. Business and investment properties may be able to depreciate the system under ATO rules — check with your accountant.

faqs.q: What's the average installed cost for NSW in 2026?
faqs.a: $1,100 per kW after STC rebate is a fair benchmark. So 6.6kW ≈ $7,260, 10kW ≈ $11,000.

faqs.q: Does paying more for premium brands pay off?
faqs.a: Often yes. Tier 1 panels and reputable inverters cost 10–20% more but offer better reliability, 25-year panel warranties, and stronger performance.

faqs.q: Do micro-inverters cost more?
faqs.a: Yes, roughly $1,500–$3,000 more than a string inverter on a 6.6kW system. They're worth it for shaded or complex roofs.

faqs.q: What's the cheapest reliable system I can buy?
faqs.a: A 6.6kW system with Tier 1 panels, a Sungrow string inverter, and CEC installation typically runs $6,800–$7,500 installed after rebate.

faqs.q: Can I import panels to save money?
faqs.a: Technically yes but not worth it — imported panels without CEC approval are ineligible for STCs, voiding the rebate and often warranties.

faqs.q: Will prices drop further in the next few years?
faqs.a: Panel prices may ease slightly. But with the STC rebate dropping each year, the total installed cost is expected to stay roughly flat or rise from 2027.

faqs.q: How long are quotes valid for?
faqs.a: Typically 30 days, since STC prices and supplier pricing shift. Ask your installer to lock in pricing when you sign.

faqs.q: Are quotes tax-deductible for home offices?
faqs.a: If you claim a home office, a portion of the system may be claimable. Talk to your accountant.

faqs.q: Does Coastal Solar Co. offer fixed-price quotes?
faqs.a: Yes — we provide a full written fixed-price quote after a free site assessment with no surprises or add-ons.

faqs.q: What happens if the STC price drops before my install?
faqs.a: If your quote is locked in, your price doesn't change. If you agreed to a variable STC rate, your installer may adjust. Always ask for a fixed STC value.

faqs.q: What is the STC rebate?
faqs.a: The Small-scale Technology Certificate (STC) scheme is Australia's federal solar rebate. You receive certificates based on expected generation over a deeming period, which your installer sells for an upfront discount.

faqs.q: How much is the STC rebate worth?
faqs.a: For a 6.6kW system in Wollongong in 2026, about $2,800–$3,000 is typical. For 10kW, $4,200–$4,500.

faqs.q: How do I claim the STC rebate?
faqs.a: You don't — your installer claims it on your behalf and applies the discount at point of sale. You just sign a rebate assignment form.

faqs.q: Do I get a cheque from the government?
faqs.a: No. The STC rebate is applied as a discount off your purchase price, not a refund.

faqs.q: When does the STC rebate end?
faqs.a: It's being phased out one deeming year at a time until complete elimination on 31 December 2030.

faqs.q: How much does the rebate drop each year?
faqs.a: Roughly 20% of the remaining value. For a 6.6kW system, that's $400–$550 per year of delay.

faqs.q: Is the 2027 STC drop guaranteed?
faqs.a: Yes. It's legislated and scheduled. Expect the deeming period to drop from 5 years (2026) to 4 years (2027).

faqs.q: Does installation date or contract date determine the rebate?
faqs.a: Installation and grid connection date is what matters. You need to be commissioned before 31 December to lock in that year's rebate.

faqs.q: What is the STC 'deeming period'?
faqs.a: It's the number of years until 2030 over which your system is assumed to generate clean energy. More deeming years = more certificates = bigger rebate.

faqs.q: How is the number of STCs calculated?
faqs.a: STCs = System kW × deeming years × zone multiplier. Wollongong is Zone 3 (multiplier 1.382).

faqs.q: What is an STC spot price?
faqs.a: It's the market price paid for each certificate, typically $36–$39 each in 2026. Your installer locks this in or passes through the market rate.

faqs.q: Am I eligible for STCs?
faqs.a: Yes, if you use CEC-accredited installers, CEC-approved panels and inverters, and install a system ≤100kW.

faqs.q: Do I qualify if I install the system myself?
faqs.a: No. STCs require a CEC-accredited installer and signed-off electrical compliance. DIY solar isn't eligible.

faqs.q: Does the rebate apply to batteries?
faqs.a: Not under STCs. Batteries are covered by the NSW Cheaper Home Batteries Program (separate incentive).

faqs.q: Can I stack STC with other rebates?
faqs.a: Yes. STC + NSW Cheaper Home Batteries + feed-in tariff all stack. Together they can save $5,000–$7,000 on a solar + battery system.

faqs.q: What counts as 'system commissioning'?
faqs.a: It's the moment your system is inspected, connected, and approved by your DNSP (Endeavour or Essential Energy). Until then, you haven't locked in the rebate.

faqs.q: What is the federal solar rebate being replaced with?
faqs.a: There is no announced federal replacement. Post-2030, expect reliance on state-based rebates and feed-in tariffs.

faqs.q: Do pensioners get extra solar rebates?
faqs.a: Not federally. Some NSW programs periodically offer concession-card-based solar subsidies — ask about current eligibility.

faqs.q: Are there rebates for rental properties?
faqs.a: Landlords can claim the STC rebate on investment properties. Some states have targeted tenant programs.

faqs.q: Can I use STC if my installer isn't CEC-accredited?
faqs.a: No. CEC accreditation is mandatory for STC eligibility.

faqs.q: What if my installer loses CEC accreditation before completion?
faqs.a: Your rebate may be at risk. Choose established installers with a long track record to avoid this.

faqs.q: Can apartments claim STCs?
faqs.a: Yes, for individual units with dedicated roof/solar. Body-corp installs can also qualify if structured correctly.

faqs.q: What happens if the STC price crashes?
faqs.a: Your rebate value drops accordingly unless your installer agreed to a fixed STC price in your contract.

faqs.q: Is the STC rebate taxable income?
faqs.a: No — it's treated as a purchase discount for residential installs.

faqs.q: Do I need to register my STCs separately?
faqs.a: No. Your installer registers and sells them through an STC trader or agent. You sign one assignment form and that's it.

faqs.q: What is the Cheaper Home Batteries Program?
faqs.a: A NSW government rebate that provides upfront discounts on approved home batteries — up to $2,000 per household — launched in late 2024.

faqs.q: Who is eligible for the battery rebate?
faqs.a: NSW homeowners with eligible rooftop solar, using an accredited installer and approved battery product. Renters need landlord consent.

faqs.q: How much is the battery rebate worth?
faqs.a: Approximately $300–$400 per usable kWh, capped at $2,000 per household. A 10kWh Tesla Powerwall typically attracts the full $2,000.

faqs.q: Does the battery rebate stack with STCs?
faqs.a: Yes. The STC rebate applies to solar and the Cheaper Home Batteries Program applies to batteries — they combine on hybrid installs.

faqs.q: How do I apply for the rebate?
faqs.a: Your accredited installer applies on your behalf. You simply sign a rebate assignment form and the discount is taken off your quote.

faqs.q: Can I add a battery to existing solar and get the rebate?
faqs.a: Yes. The rebate doesn't require a new solar install — adding a battery to existing solar also qualifies.

faqs.q: Which batteries are eligible?
faqs.a: Only CEC-approved batteries, including Tesla Powerwall, BYD HVM, Sungrow SBR, Enphase IQ 5P, LG RESU Prime and other approved models.

faqs.q: When does the Cheaper Home Batteries Program end?
faqs.a: Current funding runs through 2027 with periodic reviews. Apply as soon as practical — funding has been extended before but isn't guaranteed indefinitely.

faqs.q: Is there a cap on battery size?
faqs.a: The rebate is capped at $2,000 per household regardless of battery size — so smaller batteries get full rebate coverage but larger ones don't scale.

faqs.q: Can I claim the rebate twice on the same property?
faqs.a: No. The rebate is once per household address.

faqs.q: Do landlords get the battery rebate?
faqs.a: Yes, if the installation is completed on a property they own, with an eligible installer.

faqs.q: What happens if I move house after claiming?
faqs.a: The rebate stays with the system — there's no clawback if you sell the property.

faqs.q: Does the rebate cover installation?
faqs.a: Yes — the rebate is applied against the total installed cost, including battery hardware and labour.

faqs.q: Can I combine the rebate with a Virtual Power Plant signing bonus?
faqs.a: Yes. VPP bonuses and the battery rebate are independent — most installers let you stack both.

faqs.q: What if the battery rebate decreases in 2027?
faqs.a: The rebate structure is scheduled for review. Install in 2026 to lock in current rates.

faqs.q: What is a feed-in tariff?
faqs.a: It's the rate your retailer pays you for solar energy you export to the grid, in cents per kWh. It's a credit against your bill, not a cash payment.

faqs.q: What are feed-in tariffs in NSW in 2026?
faqs.a: Typically 5–10¢/kWh, with the best rates around 7–10¢/kWh and many retailers offering just 4–6¢/kWh. Retailer plans change frequently.

faqs.q: Why have feed-in tariffs dropped so much?
faqs.a: Mass solar uptake has flooded the grid mid-day, pushing wholesale prices near zero. Retailers can no longer resell solar exports profitably at the old 12–17¢ rates.

faqs.q: Which retailer has the best feed-in tariff?
faqs.a: It changes quarterly. As of early 2026, AGL, Origin, Red Energy, and some challenger brands offer the most competitive rates, often with caps on daily exports.

faqs.q: Is a high feed-in tariff always the best deal?
faqs.a: No. A high feed-in tariff often comes with a higher daily supply charge or peak rate. Always compare total quarterly cost, not just one number.

faqs.q: Do feed-in tariffs have caps?
faqs.a: Many plans cap export credits at 10–14 kWh/day. Anything above the cap earns nothing. If you have a big system, look for uncapped plans.

faqs.q: Can I get time-of-export rates?
faqs.a: Some retailers offer higher feed-in rates during evening peak export times. These 'solar sponge' tariffs usually require a smart meter and flexible billing.

faqs.q: Is the feed-in tariff the same for everyone?
faqs.a: No. Retailers compete. You can compare plans on Energy Made Easy or the AER site.

faqs.q: Do I get paid if I generate more than I use?
faqs.a: Yes, via feed-in tariff credits. Excess credits typically carry over to future bills. Some retailers pay out surplus annually.

faqs.q: What happens if I export more than I import?
faqs.a: You'll have a credit balance. Retailers can pay this out on request, depending on their policy.

faqs.q: Can the government raise feed-in tariffs again?
faqs.a: It's unlikely. The trend is downward as grid-scale solar floods wholesale markets mid-day.

faqs.q: Should I choose a high feed-in tariff plan?
faqs.a: Only if you export a lot. If you self-consume well (especially with a battery), you're better off picking low supply charge plans.

faqs.q: Do I need a special meter for feed-in tariffs?
faqs.a: Yes — a smart meter (Type 4 or Type 5). Your retailer arranges the upgrade, usually at no cost.

faqs.q: Can I negotiate my feed-in tariff?
faqs.a: Retailers rarely negotiate. Compare market rates and switch if yours becomes uncompetitive.

faqs.q: How often does my retailer review feed-in tariffs?
faqs.a: Typically quarterly or annually. Check your next bill or sign up for rate-change notifications.

faqs.q: Are there penalties for switching retailers?
faqs.a: Most NSW residential contracts have no exit fees. Check your current plan's terms of use.

faqs.q: Can I have different rates in summer vs winter?
faqs.a: Some plans offer seasonal or time-based rates. Most use flat feed-in tariffs year-round.

faqs.q: How does a VPP affect my feed-in tariff?
faqs.a: VPP-enrolled batteries may receive higher feed-in rates or sign-up bonuses but trade off some control over battery dispatch.

faqs.q: Why does my bill show different export amounts each quarter?
faqs.a: Generation varies with seasons, weather, and usage patterns. Winter exports are 30–50% lower than summer in NSW.

faqs.q: Is feed-in income taxable?
faqs.a: No for residential. Business/investment properties may need to treat it as assessable income — consult your accountant.

faqs.q: What is a Tier 1 solar panel?
faqs.a: Tier 1 is a ranking by Bloomberg NEF based on the manufacturer's bankability — not technical quality. It reflects financial stability and large-project usage. Most quality residential panels come from Tier 1 brands.

faqs.q: What are the best solar panel brands in 2026?
faqs.a: Trina, LONGi, Jinko, REC, SunPower Maxeon, JA Solar, Canadian Solar and QCells are among the most reliable Tier 1 brands sold in Australia.

faqs.q: Are Chinese panels any good?
faqs.a: Most 'Chinese' panels are Tier 1 and used on major grid-scale projects worldwide. Brand, warranty, and local support matter more than country of origin.

faqs.q: What's the difference between monocrystalline and polycrystalline?
faqs.a: Mono panels (dark, uniform cells) are more efficient and dominate the market in 2026. Poly panels (blue, patterned cells) are mostly obsolete for new residential installs.

faqs.q: What is a bifacial panel?
faqs.a: A bifacial panel produces electricity from both sides, capturing reflected light. They perform well on ground mounts and flat white roofs but offer little advantage on standard tile roofs.

faqs.q: What is a TOPCon panel?
faqs.a: TOPCon (Tunnel Oxide Passivated Contact) is a modern high-efficiency cell technology — common in premium 2026 panels. It offers ~22–23% efficiency vs 20–21% for older PERC cells.

faqs.q: What is a heterojunction (HJT) panel?
faqs.a: HJT is another high-efficiency technology offering 22–23%+ efficiency and better performance in heat. Usually used in premium panels like REC Alpha and Maxeon.

faqs.q: How efficient are modern panels?
faqs.a: Residential panels range from 19% to 23% efficiency. Premium panels like REC Alpha Pure RX hit 22.6%.

faqs.q: What wattage panels should I get?
faqs.a: In 2026, 400–440W panels are standard. 500W+ panels exist but aren't yet ideal for residential because of weight and roof-space tradeoffs.

faqs.q: Are high-watt panels better?
faqs.a: Not always. Higher watts usually mean larger panels, which may not fit awkward roofs. What matters is total system capacity, not individual panel size.

faqs.q: What is the product warranty on panels?
faqs.a: Most quality panels come with a 25-year product warranty and a 25-year performance warranty.

faqs.q: What is a performance warranty?
faqs.a: A guarantee that panels will produce at least a certain percentage of rated output after X years — typically 85% at year 25 for Tier 1 panels.

faqs.q: Can solar panels be recycled?
faqs.a: Yes. Australia is developing a panel recycling scheme. Currently, end-of-life panels are disposed of through a growing network of recyclers like Reclaim PV and SolarRecycle.

faqs.q: Do panels need special cleaning?
faqs.a: Generally no — rain handles most cleaning. Panels in dusty environments or near eucalyptus trees may benefit from annual soft-water cleaning.

faqs.q: Will bird droppings damage my panels?
faqs.a: Not structurally, but they can shade cells and reduce output. Regular light rinsing or bird-deterrent systems keep performance high.

faqs.q: Can panels handle hail?
faqs.a: Tier 1 panels are tested to withstand hail up to 25mm at 80 km/h. Most pass much higher tests in the lab.

faqs.q: Are panels waterproof?
faqs.a: Yes. Panels are IP67-rated, meaning they're sealed against dust and water immersion. They perform fine in heavy rain.

faqs.q: Can I install different panel brands on the same system?
faqs.a: Not ideal — mixing types can create electrical mismatches unless you use microinverters or power optimisers.

faqs.q: How long do panels take to install?
faqs.a: A 6.6kW system takes 4–8 hours. A 10kW system takes a full working day with a two- to three-person team.

faqs.q: Do panels need to face the sun directly?
faqs.a: For maximum output yes, but modern panels still produce well at tilts of 10°–40° and orientations within 45° of true north.

faqs.q: Can dust significantly reduce output?
faqs.a: Yes — heavy dust or bird droppings can cut output by 5–20%. Periodic cleaning recovers most losses.

faqs.q: Are thin-film panels used anymore?
faqs.a: Rarely for residential. Thin-film is used in specialised commercial and building-integrated applications.

faqs.q: What is cell shading?
faqs.a: If one cell is shaded, it can reduce output across the whole panel due to series wiring. Panels with half-cut cells and bypass diodes minimise this.

faqs.q: What are half-cut cells?
faqs.a: Panels where each cell is split in half to reduce resistance, improve efficiency, and mitigate shading losses. Standard on most 2026 panels.

faqs.q: Can I add more panels to my existing array?
faqs.a: Yes, if your inverter has capacity and the panels are electrically compatible. Otherwise it may require a new string or added microinverters.

faqs.q: Does panel age reduce output?
faqs.a: Yes — a small, predictable amount. Tier 1 panels lose 0.25–0.5% per year. Older panels (pre-2015) may degrade faster.

faqs.q: What's the warranty on budget panels?
faqs.a: Usually 10–12 years product, 20–25 years performance. Look for solid Australian support before saving money on warranty terms.

faqs.q: Are all-black panels more expensive?
faqs.a: Yes, by about 5–10%. They offer better aesthetics but similar performance to standard silver-framed panels.

faqs.q: Do panels perform well in salty coastal air?
faqs.a: Panels with aluminium frames and tempered glass handle coastal environments well. Coledale and Thirroul installs last 25+ years with proper mounting.

faqs.q: What happens if a panel fails?
faqs.a: Under warranty, it's replaced free. Your installer diagnoses via monitoring, files a claim with the manufacturer, and arranges replacement/swap.

faqs.q: What does a solar inverter do?
faqs.a: It converts DC electricity from your panels into AC electricity that powers your home and feeds the grid.

faqs.q: What is a string inverter?
faqs.a: A single central inverter connected to strings of panels. Economical and reliable for most homes but affected by partial shading on any panel in the string.

faqs.q: What are microinverters?
faqs.a: Small inverters attached to each panel individually. They maximise per-panel output and handle shading much better than string inverters.

faqs.q: What is a hybrid inverter?
faqs.a: A hybrid inverter manages both solar and battery storage in one unit. Required for DC-coupled battery systems like Sungrow SH10 + SBR.

faqs.q: What's the best inverter brand?
faqs.a: Fronius, Sungrow, SMA, Enphase and Goodwe are widely trusted in Australia. Fronius and Enphase are premium-tier; Sungrow offers the best value Tier 1.

faqs.q: Is Fronius better than Sungrow?
faqs.a: Fronius is slightly more expensive and has excellent Austrian engineering. Sungrow is often chosen for its strong hybrid capability and lower price. Both have great warranties and Australian support.

faqs.q: What is the warranty on a solar inverter?
faqs.a: Standard warranties are 5–10 years. Fronius and SMA include 10 years. Enphase microinverters carry 25-year warranties.

faqs.q: How long do inverters last?
faqs.a: String inverters typically last 12–15 years; microinverters 20–25+ years. Expect to replace your string inverter once during the 25-year life of your panels.

faqs.q: Where should my inverter be installed?
faqs.a: In a shaded, ventilated, dry location — garage walls or sheltered external walls are ideal. Avoid direct sun and living areas for noise reasons.

faqs.q: How loud are inverters?
faqs.a: Modern inverters operate at 25–45dB — quieter than a fridge. Some have small fans that run under heavy load.

faqs.q: Do inverters need maintenance?
faqs.a: Very little. Clean vents annually and check the display. Professional servicing every 3–5 years is ideal.

faqs.q: What happens if my inverter fails?
faqs.a: Your system stops producing. Replacement under warranty is typically free but may take 2–4 weeks. Good installers loan a temporary unit where possible.

faqs.q: Should I buy the biggest inverter I can?
faqs.a: Not necessarily. Oversize your panels relative to the inverter (up to 33% DC/AC ratio) for better shoulder-hour performance.

faqs.q: What's a DC/AC ratio?
faqs.a: The ratio of panel capacity to inverter capacity. 1.33 means 33% more panels than inverter — common for better year-round production.

faqs.q: Why do some installers recommend microinverters?
faqs.a: They shine on shaded roofs, complex rooflines, or safety-critical installs (aluminium frames, asbestos roofs). They also allow panel-level monitoring.

faqs.q: Do microinverters work better in partial shade?
faqs.a: Yes. Because each panel operates independently, shade on one panel doesn't reduce the output of the others.

faqs.q: Are micro-inverters worth the extra cost?
faqs.a: For shaded, complex, or high-end installs — yes. For a simple, unshaded north-facing roof, a string inverter is usually more cost-effective.

faqs.q: What is a DC isolator?
faqs.a: A safety switch that disconnects panels from the inverter. Mandatory on all Australian rooftop solar installs.

faqs.q: What is an AC isolator?
faqs.a: A safety switch between the inverter and your meter/switchboard. Also mandatory, usually installed near the switchboard.

faqs.q: Does my inverter have Wi-Fi?
faqs.a: Most modern inverters include built-in Wi-Fi for cloud monitoring via Fronius Solarweb, iSolarCloud (Sungrow), Enlighten (Enphase), etc.

faqs.q: What is a hybrid-ready inverter?
faqs.a: A hybrid inverter designed to accept battery storage now or later. Installing one today future-proofs for battery expansion.

faqs.q: Can I add a battery to an existing string inverter?
faqs.a: Yes, with an AC-coupled battery (like Tesla Powerwall 3). DC-coupled batteries usually require a hybrid inverter.

faqs.q: Is an Enphase system better than a string system?
faqs.a: Enphase is excellent for shaded roofs or future battery expansion with Enphase IQ batteries. Cost is higher but reliability and monitoring are industry-leading.

faqs.q: What's the difference between an inverter and a battery inverter?
faqs.a: A solar inverter converts DC panel output to AC. A battery inverter handles bidirectional flow between the battery and home. Hybrid inverters do both.

faqs.q: Why does my inverter turn off sometimes?
faqs.a: Likely due to over-voltage at the grid connection or a fault. Check Wi-Fi monitoring; if it persists, call your installer.

faqs.q: What is a home battery?
faqs.a: A rechargeable energy storage device that stores excess solar and dispatches it later — usually overnight — to reduce grid imports.

faqs.q: How do solar batteries work?
faqs.a: During the day, extra solar charges the battery. At night or in the morning, the battery discharges to power the home before pulling from the grid.

faqs.q: What's the difference between AC-coupled and DC-coupled batteries?
faqs.a: AC-coupled: connected to your home's AC wiring; works with any inverter. DC-coupled: connected directly to the solar inverter; slightly more efficient but usually needs a hybrid inverter.

faqs.q: Which battery is best: Tesla, BYD or Enphase?
faqs.a: Tesla Powerwall 3 leads on backup performance and all-in-one design; BYD HVM offers strong value and modular scaling; Enphase IQ 5P is best for microinverter systems and offers panel-level integration.

faqs.q: How big a battery do I need?
faqs.a: Multiply your evening (5pm–midnight) kWh usage by 1.2. For most Illawarra homes, 10–13.5 kWh is ideal.

faqs.q: Is a 10kWh battery enough for a family home?
faqs.a: For most 4-person Illawarra homes, yes — it covers 70–90% of evening consumption after a sunny day.

faqs.q: Will the battery keep my lights on during a blackout?
faqs.a: Only if it supports backup and the installer wires in a backup gateway. Not all batteries include this by default.

faqs.q: Do batteries work off-grid?
faqs.a: Some do with proper configuration (Tesla Powerwall 3, Selectronic SP Pro, Victron setups). Most grid-tied batteries only provide partial backup.

faqs.q: How long do batteries last?
faqs.a: Modern LFP batteries carry 10-year warranties and are expected to deliver 70–80% capacity after that. Real-world lifespan is 12–15+ years.

faqs.q: What is a battery cycle?
faqs.a: One cycle = full charge + full discharge. Most home batteries are warranted for ~3,650–10,000 cycles depending on chemistry.

faqs.q: What is throughput warranty?
faqs.a: Total kWh a battery can deliver over its life before warranty runs out. E.g., Tesla Powerwall 3 is unlimited throughput for 10 years.

faqs.q: What is battery chemistry?
faqs.a: Home batteries use either lithium iron phosphate (LFP) — safer, longer-lasting — or NMC lithium ion — more energy-dense but less stable in heat.

faqs.q: Is LFP safer than NMC?
faqs.a: Yes. LFP is chemically stable and doesn't suffer thermal runaway under fault conditions. LFP dominates 2026 home batteries.

faqs.q: How long to charge a battery from solar?
faqs.a: A 10kWh battery fully charges in 2–3 hours of full sun from a 6.6kW+ system.

faqs.q: Can I charge the battery from the grid?
faqs.a: Yes, most modern batteries allow this. Useful for time-of-use plans where off-peak grid charging + peak discharge saves money.

faqs.q: How much does a battery save me?
faqs.a: Typical savings are $1,000–$1,600/year in bill reduction for a 10kWh battery in NSW, depending on usage patterns.

faqs.q: How long does a battery take to pay back?
faqs.a: 7–9 years for most South Coast homes in 2026, after Cheaper Home Batteries Program rebate.

faqs.q: Are second-hand batteries safe?
faqs.a: Risky. Batteries degrade unpredictably, and warranties rarely transfer. Buy new for residential use.

faqs.q: Can batteries be installed indoors?
faqs.a: Most can't be installed in bedrooms or living areas under AS/NZS 5139. Garages, laundries, and protected external walls are preferred.

faqs.q: What is AS/NZS 5139?
faqs.a: The Australian Standard for battery installation. It dictates location, ventilation, fire separation, and signage. Non-compliant installs void warranties and home insurance.

faqs.q: Do batteries need ventilation?
faqs.a: Yes — batteries need airflow and minimum clearances. Some require additional exhaust or must be in non-combustible enclosures.

faqs.q: What if I already have solar — can I add a battery?
faqs.a: Yes. AC-coupled batteries retrofit to any existing solar with no inverter change. DC-coupled may require a hybrid inverter upgrade.

faqs.q: How many batteries can I install?
faqs.a: Depends on brand. Tesla Powerwall 3 allows up to 4 units. BYD HVM scales up to ~22 kWh per stack with multiple stacks possible.

faqs.q: Can batteries power heavy loads like air conditioners?
faqs.a: Yes, if the continuous discharge rate matches the load. Tesla Powerwall 3 delivers 11.5 kW continuous; BYD HVM delivers 9 kW.

faqs.q: Will my EV charge from my battery?
faqs.a: Technically possible but not recommended for primary EV charging. Most owners charge EVs direct from solar during the day.

faqs.q: Is a battery worth it if I'm on a high feed-in tariff?
faqs.a: Possibly not. If you export at 15¢+/kWh, the 'spread' between export and import is smaller. Run the maths before committing.

faqs.q: What happens when my battery is full?
faqs.a: Excess solar exports to the grid (earning feed-in tariff) or is curtailed depending on your setup.

faqs.q: What is battery throttling?
faqs.a: Reduced output in extreme temperatures. Most batteries throttle above 40°C or below 0°C — important for outdoor installations in summer.

faqs.q: Do batteries work in winter?
faqs.a: Yes, though you may need to draw more from the grid due to shorter solar days. Batteries still cycle daily but may not reach 100% charge in winter.

faqs.q: What's the cheapest reliable battery?
faqs.a: In 2026, Sungrow SBR and BYD HVM offer the best $/kWh for Tier 1 LFP. A 10kWh Sungrow system typically runs $7,500–$9,500 after rebate.

faqs.q: Is Tesla Powerwall 3 worth the premium?
faqs.a: Yes if you want integrated inverter, backup, and best-in-class monitoring. No if you just want bulk storage at the lowest price.

faqs.q: Can I use solar batteries for time-of-use arbitrage?
faqs.a: Yes — charge with cheap grid power off-peak, discharge during peak. Works especially well with time-of-use retail plans in NSW.

faqs.q: Do batteries degrade if I don't use them?
faqs.a: Yes, slowly. LFP batteries degrade less when idle than NMC. Cycling them regularly actually extends life compared to letting them sit full.

faqs.q: What is a VPP-enabled battery?
faqs.a: A battery connected to a Virtual Power Plant — an aggregator that discharges your battery when grid prices are high, paying you a share of the revenue.

faqs.q: Should I join a VPP?
faqs.a: Yes if the sign-on bonus and dispatch payments outweigh control loss. No if you need full backup control (e.g., medical equipment, home office).

faqs.q: Do batteries come with monitoring apps?
faqs.a: Yes — Tesla app, iSolarCloud (Sungrow), Enlighten (Enphase), Alpha Cloud (Alpha ESS), and more.

faqs.q: Can I expand my battery later?
faqs.a: Most modern batteries support modular expansion (BYD, Sungrow, Enphase). Tesla Powerwall expands by adding extra units.

faqs.q: Do I need an electrician to install a battery?
faqs.a: Yes — a CEC-accredited battery installer is legally required in Australia.

faqs.q: Are batteries covered by home insurance?
faqs.a: Most policies cover home batteries but check your PDS. Some insurers require notification and proof of AS/NZS 5139 compliance.

faqs.q: What's the warranty process for batteries?
faqs.a: Your installer diagnoses via monitoring, files a claim with the manufacturer, and swaps the unit at no cost within warranty.

faqs.q: How long does solar installation take?
faqs.a: A 6.6kW install takes one working day; 10kW takes 1–1.5 days; 13kW+ takes 1.5–2 days. Most installs are complete in a single appointment.

faqs.q: Do I need to be home during installation?
faqs.a: Yes, at the start and end. You'll need to meet the installer, grant roof access, and sign off when complete.

faqs.q: Will installation damage my roof?
faqs.a: Not when done properly. CEC-accredited installers use approved mounting systems with waterproof flashing. Warranty covers any leaks from workmanship.

faqs.q: Can I install solar on a tile roof?
faqs.a: Yes. Tile roofs take slightly longer than tin, and broken tiles (if any) are replaced during the install.

faqs.q: Can I install solar on a tin roof?
faqs.a: Yes. Tin is the fastest, easiest roof type for solar mounting.

faqs.q: Will solar panels leak my roof?
faqs.a: Properly flashed mounts won't leak. If anything leaks within the workmanship warranty (typically 10 years), your installer rectifies it at no cost.

faqs.q: How do I prepare for installation day?
faqs.a: Ensure roof access, move valuables away from the switchboard area, and ensure power is available for testing. Your installer will brief you beforehand.

faqs.q: Will the power go off during installation?
faqs.a: Yes, briefly — typically 1–2 hours while the installer connects the inverter and switchboard. Plan meals and work-from-home needs accordingly.

faqs.q: Do I need scaffolding?
faqs.a: For single-storey homes usually not. Two-storey or steep roofs may need scaffolding ($200–$800), typically included in the quote.

faqs.q: Who connects my solar to the grid?
faqs.a: Your installer submits a connection application to Endeavour or Essential Energy. Once approved, the meter is swapped and the system is commissioned.

faqs.q: How long does grid connection approval take?
faqs.a: Typically 1–3 weeks in Endeavour Energy areas; 2–4 weeks for Essential Energy. Larger systems may take longer.

faqs.q: What is system commissioning?
faqs.a: The final step where the installer tests the system, verifies performance, and activates grid export. Your rebate is locked in at this point.

faqs.q: What is a certificate of compliance?
faqs.a: A government-issued document certifying your solar install meets electrical standards. Your installer issues and lodges it automatically.

faqs.q: Do I need a building permit for solar?
faqs.a: Generally no — solar is exempt development under NSW SEPP. Heritage properties may need council approval.

faqs.q: Can installation be done in rainy weather?
faqs.a: Light rain, yes. Heavy rain delays for safety. Your installer will reschedule without charge if the weather turns severe.

faqs.q: Will I need to upgrade my meter?
faqs.a: Yes, to a smart meter if you don't already have one. Your retailer typically supplies and installs it at no upfront cost.

faqs.q: Do I need to upgrade my switchboard?
faqs.a: Some older switchboards (pre-2000) need upgrading. A site inspection confirms. Upgrades run $800–$1,800.

faqs.q: What happens after installation?
faqs.a: Testing, commissioning, DNSP approval, meter swap, monitoring setup, and final handover. Your installer walks you through app setup and basic operation.

faqs.q: How do I know my system is working correctly?
faqs.a: Via monitoring app. Your inverter will show live output, daily totals, and fault alerts. Your installer will check the first 7 days remotely.

faqs.q: What if my system has issues after install?
faqs.a: Call your installer. CEC-accredited installers must service warranty issues free of charge. Good installers offer remote diagnostics via monitoring.

faqs.q: How soon after installation will I see bill savings?
faqs.a: Immediately on your next quarterly bill, though full savings are visible after one full billing cycle (3 months).

faqs.q: Will my installer fill out paperwork for me?
faqs.a: Yes. CEC compliance, STC rebate, DNSP connection, and meter upgrade requests are all handled by your installer.

faqs.q: Do I need to notify my home insurer?
faqs.a: Yes. Most insurers just note the addition; some increase premiums by 1–3%. Non-notification can void your policy.

faqs.q: What do I sign on installation day?
faqs.a: Completion certificate, STC rebate assignment form (if not pre-signed), and a connection application.

faqs.q: Can I take installation photos?
faqs.a: Yes — and you should. Photos help with any future warranty claims.

faqs.q: Do I need council approval for solar in Wollongong?
faqs.a: Most installs are exempt development under NSW SEPP (Infrastructure) 2007. Only heritage-listed properties typically need DA or CDC.

faqs.q: What about Shoalhaven Council?
faqs.a: Same exemption applies. Heritage and environmentally sensitive zones may need council sign-off.

faqs.q: What if my home is heritage-listed?
faqs.a: You'll need a DA or CDC. Allow 2–8 weeks for heritage council review. Your installer can help with the application.

faqs.q: Are there building setback rules?
faqs.a: Yes. Panels must not project above the roof ridge, and must maintain minimum setbacks from property boundaries. Your installer handles this.

faqs.q: Can I install solar on a strata building?
faqs.a: Yes, with approval from the strata committee. A special resolution may be required; some Illawarra buildings have pre-approved guidelines.

faqs.q: Do I need planning approval for batteries?
faqs.a: Generally no. Batteries are exempt development if installed per AS/NZS 5139. Some councils require notification in fire-prone zones.

faqs.q: Do I need approval for ground-mounted solar?
faqs.a: Ground mounts over 5kW or above a certain size threshold may need DA. Check with your local council.

faqs.q: Does the state have rural zone exemptions?
faqs.a: Most rural zones allow solar as exempt development up to substantial sizes. Large off-grid or farm systems may need DA.

faqs.q: Can I install solar on a heritage conservation area home?
faqs.a: Yes, usually — but panels may need to be invisible from the street. Consult council before committing.

faqs.q: What happens if I install solar without approval?
faqs.a: Unapproved installs can trigger stop-work orders, fines, or forced removal. Your installer should confirm exemption before quoting.

faqs.q: Are there rules for neighbours and solar?
faqs.a: NSW doesn't mandate 'solar access', but courts may consider impact on neighbouring solar in planning disputes.

faqs.q: Is my installer responsible for obtaining approvals?
faqs.a: Yes. A good installer handles all DNSP, rebate, and council paperwork.

faqs.q: Can I install on a rental property?
faqs.a: Yes. Landlords make the decision; tenants need consent. Some investor clients treat solar as a capital improvement.

faqs.q: What if my home is in a bushfire zone?
faqs.a: Solar installs must meet AS 3959 bushfire standards. Battery installs face additional constraints — check with your local council.

faqs.q: Do I need council approval to add a battery?
faqs.a: Generally no. Battery installs follow AS/NZS 5139 and are typically exempt development statewide.

faqs.q: What is a DNSP?
faqs.a: Distributed Network Service Provider — your 'poles and wires' company. In NSW: Endeavour Energy (Wollongong/Illawarra/western Sydney), Essential Energy (regional NSW), and Ausgrid (Sydney/central coast).

faqs.q: Who is my DNSP in the Illawarra?
faqs.a: Endeavour Energy covers the Illawarra — Wollongong, Shellharbour, Kiama and Wollondilly.

faqs.q: What about Shoalhaven and the South Coast?
faqs.a: Essential Energy covers Nowra, Berry and most of the Shoalhaven area.

faqs.q: How do I apply to connect solar to the grid?
faqs.a: Your installer submits the application on your behalf. You'll need your NMI number and some property details.

faqs.q: What is an NMI?
faqs.a: National Metering Identifier — a 10 or 11-digit number identifying your property's electricity connection. It's on every bill.

faqs.q: How long does DNSP approval take?
faqs.a: Endeavour Energy: 1–3 weeks. Essential Energy: 2–4 weeks. Complex sites or 3-phase installs may take longer.

faqs.q: What is pre-approval?
faqs.a: An informal assessment before installation. Useful for large or constrained installs. Your installer organises this.

faqs.q: What is a connection agreement?
faqs.a: A formal document between you and the DNSP specifying connection terms, export limits, and protection settings.

faqs.q: Can I install solar without grid approval?
faqs.a: No. Grid-connected systems require DNSP approval before commissioning. You can install panels but can't operate or export until approved.

faqs.q: What export limits apply?
faqs.a: Single-phase homes are usually capped at 5kW export; three-phase up to 15kW. Some constrained areas require further limiting.

faqs.q: What if my export is limited?
faqs.a: Your installer sets the limit on the inverter. You can still self-consume all solar — only exports are capped.

faqs.q: What is a dynamic export limit?
faqs.a: A scheme (DoE) where your inverter communicates with the DNSP and adjusts exports in real-time. Rolling out across NSW from 2025.

faqs.q: Do I need 3-phase for 10kW+?
faqs.a: Not necessarily. Single-phase 10kW is allowed by Endeavour Energy subject to approval. Three-phase makes 15kW+ installs easier.

faqs.q: What if my home has single-phase and I want 13kW?
faqs.a: You can either upgrade to 3-phase (expensive: $3,000–$10,000) or install single-phase with export limiting to 5kW.

faqs.q: How much does a 3-phase upgrade cost?
faqs.a: Typically $3,000–$10,000 depending on distance to transformer, street work required, and Endeavour Energy approvals.

faqs.q: Can I install solar on my off-grid property?
faqs.a: Yes — off-grid installs skip the DNSP process entirely but require batteries and generator backup.

faqs.q: Do I need a pre-installation site visit?
faqs.a: Yes. Your installer measures roof, inspects switchboard, confirms connection type, and checks for shading.

faqs.q: Will my supply be interrupted during commissioning?
faqs.a: Typically only for 1–2 hours while the meter is swapped and the inverter is connected.

faqs.q: Does the DNSP inspect my installation?
faqs.a: They verify compliance via paperwork and periodic spot audits. Physical inspections are rare for residential.

faqs.q: What happens if connection is denied?
faqs.a: Your installer works with the DNSP to remediate — usually reducing export or adjusting system size. Denials are rare for standard residential installs.

faqs.q: Do solar systems come with monitoring?
faqs.a: Yes. Every quality 2026 install includes Wi-Fi monitoring via the inverter manufacturer's cloud portal and mobile app.

faqs.q: Can I monitor my solar on my phone?
faqs.a: Yes. Fronius Solarweb, iSolarCloud (Sungrow), Enphase Enlighten, Tesla app — all provide real-time and historical data.

faqs.q: How do I know if my system is underperforming?
faqs.a: Compare actual vs expected generation in your monitoring app. Most apps flag drops automatically. Your installer can diagnose via remote monitoring.

faqs.q: What is a performance ratio?
faqs.a: The ratio of actual output vs theoretical max — a measure of system efficiency. Good systems run at 75–85%.

faqs.q: What affects system performance?
faqs.a: Panel orientation, tilt, shading, temperature, dust, inverter efficiency, and wiring losses.

faqs.q: How do I know my panels are clean?
faqs.a: Check monitoring for unexplained output drops. Visually inspect panels yearly for bird droppings, dust, or debris.

faqs.q: What's a normal daily generation for 6.6kW?
faqs.a: In the Illawarra: 26–29kWh/day on average, 18–22kWh in winter, 33–40kWh in summer.

faqs.q: What's a normal daily generation for 10kW?
faqs.a: 40–44kWh/day on average, 28–33kWh in winter, 50–60kWh in summer.

faqs.q: Why is my solar generating less than expected?
faqs.a: Common causes: shading from trees/new buildings, inverter fault, dirty panels, cloudy weather, or panel degradation. Check monitoring first.

faqs.q: What should my system produce in summer vs winter?
faqs.a: Summer: typically 130–150% of annual average. Winter: 60–70% of annual average.

faqs.q: What is MPPT?
faqs.a: Maximum Power Point Tracking — inverter technology that optimises panel output under varying conditions. All modern inverters have 2+ MPPT channels.

faqs.q: How often should I check my monitoring?
faqs.a: Weekly is ideal for the first 3 months, then monthly. Set up email alerts for faults to avoid unnoticed downtime.

faqs.q: What if my monitoring app shows no data?
faqs.a: Likely a Wi-Fi dropout or inverter comms issue. Reboot the inverter, check the router, and contact your installer if persistent.

faqs.q: Can I monitor individual panels?
faqs.a: Yes, with microinverters (Enphase) or DC optimisers (SolarEdge). String inverter systems show string-level data only.

faqs.q: What is solar yield?
faqs.a: Energy produced per kW installed per year (kWh/kW/year). Illawarra averages 1,450–1,550 kWh/kW.

faqs.q: Why does my output drop at peak midday?
faqs.a: High temperatures reduce panel efficiency. On 35°C+ days, output can drop 10–15% from theoretical max.

faqs.q: What are panel-level shutdowns?
faqs.a: Safety feature on microinverter/optimiser systems — panels de-energize if the inverter fails, making rescues safer.

faqs.q: Can I export my data to a CSV?
faqs.a: Yes. Most monitoring platforms export historical data for analysis or accounting.

faqs.q: Is my monitoring data private?
faqs.a: Your installer can see your data; manufacturer sees aggregate data. Review the platform's privacy policy for specifics.

faqs.q: How do I know if my system is faulty vs just a cloudy day?
faqs.a: Compare to past days with similar weather. Sudden drops in line-of-sight weather usually indicate a fault.

faqs.q: Do solar panels need cleaning?
faqs.a: Usually rain handles it. Panels in heavily dusty, coastal, or tree-shaded locations benefit from annual cleaning.

faqs.q: How often should I clean my panels?
faqs.a: Every 12 months is typical. More often if near gum trees, bird roosts, or a coastal environment.

faqs.q: Can I clean my panels myself?
faqs.a: Yes — with soft water, a soft brush, and proper safety gear. For roofs over 15°, hiring a professional is safer.

faqs.q: What cleaning products are safe?
faqs.a: Water, mild detergent, or dedicated solar panel cleaner. Avoid abrasive pads, harsh chemicals, and high-pressure washers.

faqs.q: Can I use a pressure washer?
faqs.a: No. Pressure washers can damage panel seals and void warranties.

faqs.q: How much does professional cleaning cost?
faqs.a: $150–$400 for a typical 6.6–10kW residential system in NSW.

faqs.q: Do panels need annual servicing?
faqs.a: A professional inspection every 2–3 years checks mounting, cabling, inverter fans, and seals. Most issues are caught via monitoring instead.

faqs.q: What maintenance do inverters need?
faqs.a: Very little. Clean intake vents annually and ensure clearance from walls. Fans may need cleaning every 3–5 years.

faqs.q: Do batteries need maintenance?
faqs.a: Virtually none for modern LFP batteries. Firmware updates are automatic. Check app for alerts.

faqs.q: What's a typical service cost?
faqs.a: $200–$400 for a full inspection including panel cleaning, cable check, and inverter diagnostics.

faqs.q: Do I need a roof inspection after 10 years?
faqs.a: Recommended. Panels shield your roof, but mounting points and flashings should be checked for seal integrity.

faqs.q: Can trees damage my solar?
faqs.a: Overhanging branches can cause shading, leaf buildup, and impact damage from falling limbs. Trim trees at install and every 2 years.

faqs.q: What if panels are loose?
faqs.a: Contact your installer immediately. Loose panels are a safety risk and should be tightened under warranty.

faqs.q: Do I need to do anything in storm season?
faqs.a: Monitor for debris after storms. Panels can handle hail and wind; broken tiles near mounts should be replaced promptly.

faqs.q: Should I turn off my system during a storm?
faqs.a: No. Panels and inverters are designed for storm conditions. Turning off doesn't add protection.

faqs.q: What happens if hail damages my panels?
faqs.a: Your home insurance covers hail damage. Some manufacturers also cover hail within certain parameters — check the product warranty.

faqs.q: Do I need lightning protection?
faqs.a: Most Australian inverters have built-in surge protection. If your home is in a high-lightning zone, additional external surge protection costs $150–$400.

faqs.q: How do I safely shut down my system?
faqs.a: Flip the AC isolator, then the DC isolator. Your installer should show you during handover.

faqs.q: Do solar panels attract birds or pests?
faqs.a: The gap under panels can attract pigeons or possums. Anti-bird mesh (installed at install time) prevents nesting — about $100–$300 extra.

faqs.q: What's covered in a typical service?
faqs.a: Panel inspection, cleaning, mounting check, cable integrity, inverter fan cleaning, connection resistance test, and monitoring verification.

faqs.q: What warranties come with a solar system?
faqs.a: Panel product warranty (usually 25 years), panel performance warranty (25 years), inverter warranty (5–10 years), battery warranty (10 years), and workmanship warranty (5–10 years).

faqs.q: What is a performance warranty?
faqs.a: A guarantee that panels will produce a minimum percentage of their rated output at a given year. Typically 85% at year 25.

faqs.q: What is a workmanship warranty?
faqs.a: The installer's guarantee on installation quality — waterproofing, mounting, cabling. Minimum 5 years in Australia; top installers offer 10–15 years.

faqs.q: Is Coastal Solar Co.'s workmanship warranty transferable?
faqs.a: Yes, our workmanship warranty transfers with the home provided the new owner is notified.

faqs.q: Does the CEC warranty cover me?
faqs.a: No. The CEC accredits installers; warranty is between you, the manufacturer, and the installer.

faqs.q: What is a Clean Energy Council-approved product?
faqs.a: Products listed on the CEC-approved product list. Only these qualify for STC rebates.

faqs.q: What happens if my installer goes out of business?
faqs.a: Panel and inverter manufacturers cover their own product warranties regardless. Another CEC installer can service claims. Workmanship warranties typically die with the original installer.

faqs.q: Does my warranty include labour for replacements?
faqs.a: Depends on the product. Premium brands (REC, Enphase, Fronius, LG) often cover labour. Standard warranties usually cover product only.

faqs.q: How do I claim a warranty?
faqs.a: Contact your installer first. They diagnose via monitoring, file the claim with the manufacturer, and arrange replacement/repair.

faqs.q: What's the battery warranty on a Tesla Powerwall?
faqs.a: 10 years, unlimited throughput, 70% retained capacity minimum. Tesla arranges replacement via local service partners.

faqs.q: What is 'throughput' in a battery warranty?
faqs.a: Total energy delivered over the battery's life. Most batteries are warranted for unlimited throughput for 10 years; older designs had throughput caps (~30MWh).

faqs.q: Does the STC rebate affect my warranty?
faqs.a: No. STCs are a government incentive and don't alter product warranties.

faqs.q: Does installing my own battery void the warranty?
faqs.a: Yes. Warranties require CEC-accredited installation to remain valid.

faqs.q: How long should my inverter warranty be?
faqs.a: At least 5 years, ideally 10. Fronius and SMA include 10 years standard; Sungrow offers 10 with registration; Enphase includes 25 years.

faqs.q: Are extended warranties worth it?
faqs.a: Usually not. Factory warranties are long. Spend the money on a reputable installer instead.

faqs.q: What happens if my panels degrade faster than warranted?
faqs.a: The manufacturer replaces or compensates. Claims require documented monitoring data — a benefit of cloud monitoring from day one.

faqs.q: Is consumer law additional to warranty?
faqs.a: Yes. Australian Consumer Law (ACL) provides statutory rights that override warranty terms — you're entitled to repair/replacement for a reasonable lifetime.

faqs.q: What should my warranty document include?
faqs.a: Product details, serial numbers, warranty durations, exclusions, claim process, contact info for both installer and manufacturer.

faqs.q: Can I sue if a warranty isn't honoured?
faqs.a: Yes, under ACL. NCAT and Fair Trading can help with small disputes; larger claims go to court.

faqs.q: How do I keep my warranty valid?
faqs.a: Use the system as intended, have it serviced if required, and keep all paperwork. Some manufacturers require online registration.

faqs.q: What is the payback period for solar?
faqs.a: In the Illawarra in 2026, a quality 6.6kW system pays back in 4–6 years; 10kW in 4–6 years; 10kW + battery in 7–9 years.

faqs.q: How do I calculate payback?
faqs.a: Net system cost ÷ (annual savings). Annual savings = self-consumed kWh × retail rate + exported kWh × feed-in tariff.

faqs.q: What's the lifetime return on solar?
faqs.a: A 6.6kW system saves $70,000–$110,000 over 25 years in NSW, depending on usage patterns and electricity price rises.

faqs.q: Does solar beat putting money in the bank?
faqs.a: Usually yes. Solar typically returns 15–25% per year (as bill savings) — far better than term deposits or most investments.

faqs.q: What IRR does solar deliver?
faqs.a: A typical NSW install has an internal rate of return of 15–22% tax-free, based on bill savings vs upfront cost.

faqs.q: How does battery ROI compare to solar ROI?
faqs.a: Battery ROI is typically 10–14% — lower than solar alone but still strong vs most investment options.

faqs.q: Do rising electricity prices improve payback?
faqs.a: Yes significantly. NSW retail prices have risen ~4% per year; factoring this shortens payback by ~6–12 months.

faqs.q: How do I factor in inflation?
faqs.a: Use nominal savings (today's dollars) and add 3–4% per year to reflect price rises. Real returns are ~12–15% after inflation.

faqs.q: Is solar a good hedge against energy prices?
faqs.a: Yes. Solar converts a variable expense (grid electricity) into a fixed asset (your panels). It's one of the best retail hedges available.

faqs.q: Does solar help my household budget?
faqs.a: Yes. Most homes see quarterly bills drop 60–80% after solar install, saving $400–$800 per quarter.

faqs.q: When do I break even?
faqs.a: When cumulative savings equal your net system cost. For 6.6kW in the Illawarra, that's usually in year 4–5.

faqs.q: What happens after payback?
faqs.a: Every kWh generated is free money. After payback, most systems save $4,000–$5,500/year for 20 more years.

faqs.q: Do I still save if I move house?
faqs.a: Solar typically adds $2,000–$8,000 to resale value. You also benefit from savings during ownership.

faqs.q: Is payback better with or without a battery?
faqs.a: Without a battery, payback is faster. With a battery, total lifetime savings are higher but break-even takes longer.

faqs.q: How do I compare ROI between 6.6kW and 10kW?
faqs.a: Run both through the same payback calculator. Usually payback is similar; 10kW has larger lifetime savings.

faqs.q: Is finance-based solar still profitable?
faqs.a: Yes if the financing rate is below ~8%. Most green loans sit at 6–8%, so bill savings exceed loan payments from day one.

faqs.q: What's the break-even for a battery?
faqs.a: 7–9 years for a 10kWh battery in NSW in 2026 after the Cheaper Home Batteries Program rebate.

faqs.q: Do I need a spreadsheet to calculate ROI?
faqs.a: No — our free Solar Savings Calculator does it for you using your bill and postcode.

faqs.q: Does the STC rebate get included in ROI?
faqs.a: Yes. ROI is always calculated on net cost — the price after STC and other rebates.

faqs.q: What's the best way to increase ROI?
faqs.a: Maximise self-consumption (run appliances during sunny hours), size the system for your 5-year future needs, and add a battery when feed-in tariffs drop further.

faqs.q: How much will solar reduce my bill?
faqs.a: Typical NSW savings: 6.6kW cuts quarterly bills by 50–70%; 10kW by 65–85%; 10kW + battery by 80–95%.

faqs.q: Can my bill go to zero?
faqs.a: Very close, but not usually zero. Daily supply charges ($1–$1.50/day) still apply even if you export enough to cover usage.

faqs.q: Can I get paid by my retailer?
faqs.a: Yes, if you export more than you import and daily supply charges. Credits typically carry over each quarter; some retailers pay out annually on request.

faqs.q: What's a typical NSW residential electricity bill?
faqs.a: For a 4-person home: $500–$800 per quarter. Solar usually brings this down to $100–$300.

faqs.q: How does time-of-use affect savings?
faqs.a: Time-of-use plans penalise evening usage (peak rates up to 45–55¢/kWh). Batteries become more valuable under these plans.

faqs.q: Should I switch to time-of-use with solar?
faqs.a: Only if you have a battery or can shift most usage to daylight hours. Otherwise, flat-rate plans often work out better.

faqs.q: What's the best NSW retailer for solar?
faqs.a: Depends on your usage and feed-in tariff priority. In 2026, AGL, Origin, Red Energy and some smaller providers consistently rank well.

faqs.q: How do I compare plans?
faqs.a: Use energymadeeasy.gov.au — enter your postcode and bill and it ranks real plans for you.

faqs.q: Will my supply charge increase with solar?
faqs.a: No. Supply charges are unrelated to solar — they cover grid access and network costs.

faqs.q: Does solar reduce my winter bill too?
faqs.a: Yes, but less than summer. Winter solar generation is typically 60–70% of summer, so savings are proportionally smaller.

faqs.q: What's the maximum I can save?
faqs.a: With a 10kW + 13kWh battery in Wollongong, many homes reduce their annual electricity spend from $3,000 to $200–$400.

faqs.q: How does an EV affect solar savings?
faqs.a: An EV charged at home uses 3,000–5,000 kWh/year. Solar turns that 'fuel' cost from ~$1,200 into ~$300 over a year.

faqs.q: Can I charge multiple appliances from solar?
faqs.a: Yes. Running dishwasher, pool pump, washing machine, and EV charger during daylight hours maximises self-consumption.

faqs.q: What is self-consumption?
faqs.a: The percentage of solar energy you use directly at home instead of exporting. Higher self-consumption = bigger bill savings.

faqs.q: How do I increase self-consumption?
faqs.a: Run heavy appliances midday, pre-cool or heat the home, use timers on hot water, consider a battery, or buy an EV.

faqs.q: Do smart plugs help maximise solar?
faqs.a: Yes. Smart plugs can trigger appliances (aircon, dryer, EV) when solar output exceeds a threshold — boosting self-consumption by 10–15%.

faqs.q: Can I get a refund from my retailer?
faqs.a: Yes, if you're in credit. Most retailers pay on request; some pay automatically over $50 credit.

faqs.q: What's peak vs off-peak?
faqs.a: Peak: weekdays 3pm–9pm (highest cost). Shoulder: 7am–3pm weekdays. Off-peak: nights and weekends (lowest).

faqs.q: When should I use my washing machine for best savings?
faqs.a: Midday on sunny days — free solar power. Avoid evening peak 3pm–9pm if you're on time-of-use.

faqs.q: Does solar affect my daily supply charge?
faqs.a: No. The fixed daily charge continues regardless of solar usage.

faqs.q: Is my roof suitable for solar?
faqs.a: If you have unshaded north, east, or west-facing roof space of at least 15–25 m², and a modern roof (steel, tile, or Colorbond), you're a strong candidate.

faqs.q: Can I install on an old roof?
faqs.a: If the roof needs replacement within 10 years, replace it first. Otherwise, a modern, sound roof can easily support panels for 25+ years.

faqs.q: What roof pitch is best?
faqs.a: 30°–35° is optimal in NSW. Anything from 10°–45° is acceptable with minor output variation.

faqs.q: Can I install solar on a flat roof?
faqs.a: Yes, using tilt frames. Adds $200–$500 per frame but essential for optimal generation on near-flat roofs.

faqs.q: Can I install solar on a metal roof?
faqs.a: Yes. Steel/Colorbond roofs are the easiest to install on — clamps attach directly to the profile.

faqs.q: Can I install solar on a tile roof?
faqs.a: Yes. Tile hooks replace existing tiles at mount points. Takes slightly longer than metal but performance is identical.

faqs.q: What if my roof has asbestos?
faqs.a: Disclosure required. Some installers refuse; others have qualified teams to work carefully. Never cut or damage the roof material.

faqs.q: Can I put panels on a shingle roof?
faqs.a: Yes, but rare in Australia. Shingles require special mounts and care.

faqs.q: Can I install on a skillion roof?
faqs.a: Yes. Skillion (single-slope) roofs are common in modern Illawarra homes and work well with solar.

faqs.q: Do I need to reinforce my roof?
faqs.a: Usually no. Panels add only 12–15kg/m²; most Australian roofs easily support this.

faqs.q: Can I put solar on a shed?
faqs.a: Yes. A metal or tile shed roof is often a great alternative if your main roof is shaded or poorly oriented.

faqs.q: What if my roof is too small?
faqs.a: Use higher-wattage panels, split across multiple roof faces, or consider ground-mounting (if you have land).

faqs.q: Is my garage suitable?
faqs.a: If it has the right orientation and a clear roof, absolutely. Many Illawarra customers use garage roofs when the main house is shaded.

faqs.q: Does a curved roof work?
faqs.a: Gently curved roofs can take panels. Steep curves or complex shapes may require custom mounting or reduce usable area.

faqs.q: Can I re-roof after installing solar?
faqs.a: Yes, but it's expensive — panels must be temporarily removed and reinstalled. Budget $2,000–$4,000 for this. Re-roofing before solar saves money.

faqs.q: Does shading affect solar performance?
faqs.a: Yes significantly. Even small shade patches can cut panel output by 30–80% during the shaded window.

faqs.q: How do I deal with shading?
faqs.a: Use microinverters or power optimisers to isolate shaded panels. Or, place panels only on unshaded portions of the roof.

faqs.q: Will tree shade ruin my solar?
faqs.a: Heavy shade reduces yields by 20–60%. Trimming, relocating panels, or using microinverters can mitigate.

faqs.q: Do chimneys and vents cause problems?
faqs.a: Yes — they cast shade. Design panels around them or accept slight shading if unavoidable.

faqs.q: What's the best panel orientation in NSW?
faqs.a: North is optimal. East and west are 10–20% less productive annually but spread generation across morning/afternoon.

faqs.q: Can I put panels on a south-facing roof?
faqs.a: Technically yes, but output drops 25–35%. Usually not worth it unless north/east/west isn't available.

faqs.q: Is it okay to split panels across roofs?
faqs.a: Absolutely. Modern inverters handle multiple orientations well — ideal for complex rooflines.

faqs.q: What's the ideal tilt angle in Wollongong?
faqs.a: 30°–34°. Most local roofs happen to be in this range, making them near-ideal.

faqs.q: Do winter shadows matter?
faqs.a: Yes — the sun is lower in winter, so shadows are longer. Account for winter shadows during site assessment.

faqs.q: How is shading analysed?
faqs.a: Installers use tools like Solmetric SunEye, Aurora Solar, or drone surveys to simulate shade over a year.

faqs.q: Do clouds count as 'shading'?
faqs.a: Technically yes, but they affect all panels equally and don't cause the electrical mismatch that localised shade does.

faqs.q: Will my neighbour's tree cause problems?
faqs.a: Possibly. Shading from neighbouring vegetation isn't protected in NSW law — plan accordingly or negotiate trimming.

faqs.q: Can I use half-cut panels to reduce shading losses?
faqs.a: Yes. Half-cut cells reduce shading penalty by ~50% on the affected panel compared to standard cells.

faqs.q: What about skylights?
faqs.a: They don't shade panels but may reduce usable roof area. Plan around them during design.

faqs.q: Should I trim my trees before installation?
faqs.a: Yes. Trim trees to the extent possible before install, and set a 2-year trimming schedule.

faqs.q: Can I charge my EV with solar?
faqs.a: Yes. A 10kW+ solar system can charge most EVs to full in 3–6 hours of sunshine, for almost free.

faqs.q: What size solar do I need for an EV?
faqs.a: Add at least 3kW of solar per EV in typical use. A 10kW system easily powers one EV plus a home's normal use.

faqs.q: What is a solar-aware EV charger?
faqs.a: A smart charger that modulates charging based on solar output, charging the EV only when surplus solar is available.

faqs.q: Which EV chargers support solar matching?
faqs.a: Zappi, Wallbox Pulsar Plus, Fronius Wattpilot, and Tesla Wall Connector (with smart load monitoring) all offer solar-first charging.

faqs.q: How much does it cost to install an EV charger?
faqs.a: $1,500–$3,500 for a 7kW home wallbox including installation. A Zappi or Wattpilot sits at the top of that range.

faqs.q: Can I run a 22kW 3-phase charger?
faqs.a: Only if your home has 3-phase power. Most homes have single-phase and use 7–11 kW chargers instead.

faqs.q: How much solar does an EV use per year?
faqs.a: A typical 15,000 km/year EV consumes 3,000–4,500 kWh/year — roughly the output of 2–3kW of solar.

faqs.q: Do EVs drain my solar battery?
faqs.a: If charging at night or rapidly, yes. Most owners schedule charging during daylight hours to tap solar directly.

faqs.q: Can I charge my EV with a battery?
faqs.a: Yes but inefficient. Charging the battery and then the EV incurs double conversion losses. Charge direct from solar when possible.

faqs.q: Will my bill increase with an EV?
faqs.a: If not well-managed, yes. With solar + smart charging, many households absorb EV charging with no net bill increase.

faqs.q: What's the cheapest way to charge an EV?
faqs.a: Free excess solar during the day. Off-peak grid charging at 10–15¢/kWh is the backup.

faqs.q: Do I need a larger inverter for EV charging?
faqs.a: Not specifically — EVs charge on AC from your home. A bigger solar inverter just means more available solar to charge with.

faqs.q: Are there EV-specific solar rebates?
faqs.a: No dedicated federal rebate, though some NSW programs have offered EV-charging subsidies. Check current programs.

faqs.q: Do I need a battery to maximise EV benefits?
faqs.a: Not strictly. Daytime charging often works well without a battery. Batteries become more valuable if you charge mostly at night.

faqs.q: Can I export from my EV (V2G/V2H)?
faqs.a: Some EVs (Nissan Leaf, some BYD, Kia, VW) support V2G or V2H with compatible hardware. This market is still developing in Australia.

faqs.q: What is a Virtual Power Plant (VPP)?
faqs.a: A network of home batteries controlled by an aggregator. Your battery discharges during grid emergencies, earning you revenue.

faqs.q: Which VPPs are available in NSW?
faqs.a: AGL, Origin Loop, Amber, Energy Locals, Reposit Power, Tesla Energy Plan and Social Energy are the main NSW operators in 2026.

faqs.q: How much do VPPs pay?
faqs.a: Sign-up bonuses of $500–$1,500, plus $100–$500/year in dispatch payments depending on participation.

faqs.q: Do I give up control of my battery?
faqs.a: Partially. The VPP may discharge your battery during grid events — usually a few hours per month. Most offer minimum reserve settings.

faqs.q: Do I still get backup during a blackout?
