faqs.a: Yes, if your battery is backup-capable. VPP discharges only happen during grid operation.

faqs.q: Is the Tesla Energy Plan worth it?
faqs.a: If you have a Powerwall, often yes — the feed-in tariff is high but the plan lock-in and terms can be restrictive.

faqs.q: How do I sign up for a VPP?
faqs.a: Through your retailer or aggregator. Most onboard remotely — send your system details and they configure your battery.

faqs.q: Do VPPs affect my warranty?
faqs.a: No. VPPs operate within manufacturer-approved cycle limits.

faqs.q: How does my battery get dispatched?
faqs.a: Via internet commands to your battery or inverter. The aggregator adjusts charge/discharge schedules during grid events.

faqs.q: Are VPPs safe?
faqs.a: Yes. They operate within tested dispatch limits. No safety risk to your home or battery.

faqs.q: Can I leave a VPP?
faqs.a: Most allow exit with 30 days' notice. Some plans have a minimum term (1–2 years).

faqs.q: Do VPPs drain my battery every night?
faqs.a: No. Dispatches are rare (few times per month) and usually brief.

faqs.q: Will a VPP shorten my battery's life?
faqs.a: Marginally. Most VPPs limit cycles to warranty-safe levels. Net battery degradation is minimal.

faqs.q: Do I need a specific battery for a VPP?
faqs.a: Yes — Tesla Powerwall, BYD, Enphase, Sungrow, Alpha ESS, and some others. Check your VPP's compatibility list.

faqs.q: Is a VPP more lucrative than a regular feed-in tariff?
faqs.a: Often yes, because peak grid events pay at wholesale rates 10–50× normal. But volatility is higher.

faqs.q: Can I finance solar?
faqs.a: Yes. Options include green loans, personal loans, mortgage top-ups, and interest-free solar plans from major retailers.

faqs.q: What is a green loan?
faqs.a: A home loan product with a discounted rate for solar, batteries, and other energy-efficient upgrades. Typical rates: 5.5–7.5% in 2026.

faqs.q: Which banks offer green loans?
faqs.a: Commonwealth Bank, Westpac, NAB, ANZ, Macquarie, Bendigo, and some credit unions. Rates and terms vary.

faqs.q: Is $0 upfront solar a good deal?
faqs.a: Depends on the rate. Interest-free deals often bake 10–15% into the price. Check total cost paid vs equivalent cash deal.

faqs.q: How does a solar PPA work?
faqs.a: Power Purchase Agreement — a third party installs and owns the solar; you buy power at a discounted rate. Less common in Australia now that upfront ownership is affordable.

faqs.q: Can I claim solar interest on tax?
faqs.a: Residential: no. Investment properties: yes (consult your accountant).

faqs.q: What's a typical solar loan term?
faqs.a: 5–10 years. Many customers repay from bill savings alone — 'self-funding' the system.

faqs.q: Does solar finance affect my credit?
faqs.a: Yes, like any loan. Ensure you can afford repayments even without peak savings.

faqs.q: Can I pay off a solar loan early?
faqs.a: Most solar finance has no early repayment penalty. Confirm with your lender.

faqs.q: Is a BNPL (buy now pay later) solar deal good?
faqs.a: Rarely. BNPL-branded solar deals often have poor warranties and low-tier components.

faqs.q: What are the risks of financed solar?
faqs.a: Being locked into a contract if you sell, losing the rebate if you default, or paying much more than cash equivalent.

faqs.q: Is an offset loan better than a green loan?
faqs.a: An offset can be cheaper if you're disciplined. Green loans typically have a fixed term and lower rate — simpler for most families.

faqs.q: How much can I borrow for solar?
faqs.a: Up to $20,000–$50,000 depending on the product. Most residential systems fall well within this range.

faqs.q: Does my mortgage cover solar?
faqs.a: You can usually top up your mortgage. Cheapest rate but extends the debt over 20+ years — higher total interest.

faqs.q: What's the cheapest way to pay for solar?
faqs.a: Cash if possible. If financing, a green loan at 5.5–7% is typically the best combination of rate and flexibility.

faqs.q: Does solar add value to my home?
faqs.a: Yes. Research shows solar adds $2,000–$8,000 to resale value in NSW, depending on system size and age.

faqs.q: Do I need to notify my home insurer?
faqs.a: Yes. Most insurers consider solar a permanent fixture and adjust cover accordingly. Non-notification can void your policy.

faqs.q: Will solar increase my insurance premium?
faqs.a: Usually by 1–3% to cover the added hardware. Some insurers include solar for free up to a certain value.

faqs.q: Is the battery covered by home insurance?
faqs.a: Most policies cover batteries as a permanent fixture, but some require you to declare them. Confirm with your PDS.

faqs.q: What happens if hail damages my panels?
faqs.a: Home insurance covers hail damage. Some panel manufacturers also offer limited hail cover — check the product warranty.

faqs.q: Does warranty cover storm damage?
faqs.a: Product warranties don't. Your home insurance covers storm damage. Some installers cover mounting during the workmanship warranty period.

faqs.q: Do I need public liability insurance?
faqs.a: No for residential. Commercial installs may require specific public liability cover.

faqs.q: Will solar help sell my home?
faqs.a: Yes. Homes with solar sell faster and for more in most NSW metro and coastal markets.

faqs.q: Is a battery more attractive to buyers than solar alone?
faqs.a: Yes. Listings with batteries attract more interest, especially in areas with evening peak tariffs.

faqs.q: Do buyers care about the solar age?
faqs.a: Older systems (pre-2015) are often viewed as deferred upgrades. Systems under 5 years old carry nearly full installed value.

faqs.q: Can I transfer warranty to the new owner?
faqs.a: Product warranties transfer automatically. Workmanship warranties transfer if the original installer is notified — ask at handover.

faqs.q: Will solar affect my council rates?
faqs.a: No. Solar is not capital improvement for rating purposes.

faqs.q: Is solar included in home valuations?
faqs.a: Yes, usually. Valuers assess installed cost, remaining warranty, and condition.

faqs.q: What documents should I keep for resale?
faqs.a: Invoices, CEC installer certificate, warranty paperwork, monitoring data, and any service records. A digital folder is ideal.

faqs.q: Can my insurance refuse to cover my battery?
faqs.a: Rarely, if installed to AS/NZS 5139. Some insurers flag certain storage products — check before choosing your battery.

faqs.q: Why is my solar producing less than expected?
faqs.a: Usual suspects: dirt, shading, panel fault, inverter fault, or temporary cloud cover. Check monitoring first.

faqs.q: My inverter shows a red light — what do I do?
faqs.a: Red usually means fault. Note the error code, try a restart (flip AC off, wait 5 min, flip back on), and call your installer if it persists.

faqs.q: My app isn't showing data — is my solar broken?
faqs.a: Often a Wi-Fi issue, not a solar fault. Reboot router and inverter. If still offline, check inverter display for output.

faqs.q: Inverter is beeping — what does that mean?
faqs.a: Alarm usually means overvoltage, under-voltage, overheating, or insulation fault. Note the code and contact your installer.

faqs.q: My system stopped during hot weather — why?
faqs.a: Inverters throttle at 50°C+. If it stops entirely, poor ventilation or a fan fault may be the cause.

faqs.q: Why is my bill still high with solar?
faqs.a: Possible causes: undersized system, high evening usage, poor tariff, monitoring issue, shading. A quick bill + monitoring review identifies the culprit.

faqs.q: My feed-in credit looks too low — what's happening?
faqs.a: Could be a meter issue, poor tariff, system fault, or shading. Compare your retailer report vs inverter output.

faqs.q: My quarterly bill shows an error code — what should I do?
faqs.a: Bill errors are rare but usually a retailer issue, not solar. Compare your inverter monitoring's export total against your bill's feed-in line. If they differ significantly, contact your retailer.

faqs.q: My solar stopped working completely — what now?
faqs.a: Check inverter display. If off, check AC/DC isolators. If still dead, contact installer for diagnosis — usually remote via monitoring.

faqs.q: Rain got into my inverter — what do I do?
faqs.a: Turn off AC isolator and call your installer immediately. Don't touch the inverter until a pro inspects.

faqs.q: Birds are nesting under my panels — what should I do?
faqs.a: Install bird mesh around the array (about $100–$300). Don't let nests build up — they pose fire risk.

faqs.q: My battery isn't holding charge — why?
faqs.a: Could be firmware, battery fault, or incorrect settings. Contact your installer; warranty covers diagnosis.

faqs.q: My EV charger stopped working — is it solar-related?
faqs.a: Could be if solar-matching is enabled and solar output drops. Check charger logs and solar output.

faqs.q: My monitoring shows 0 kWh — is that right?
faqs.a: If it's a sunny day, no. Call your installer — something's wrong with the inverter or Wi-Fi logging.

faqs.q: Panels look dirty after install — is that normal?
faqs.a: They should be cleaned before handover. If not, ask for a clean or wait for the next rain.

faqs.q: My inverter is very noisy — normal?
faqs.a: Moderate fan noise is normal under heavy load. Grinding or whining isn't — get it checked.

faqs.q: Error code 'insulation fault' — what is that?
faqs.a: Moisture or wiring issue on the DC side. Your installer will diagnose via error log — don't try to open the inverter yourself.

faqs.q: Some panels show lower output than others — why?
faqs.a: Common causes: shading, dirt, microinverter fault, or panel degradation. Monitoring reveals the culprit.

faqs.q: Why does my system reboot randomly?
faqs.a: Usually grid voltage fluctuations. Your installer can reconfigure the inverter's voltage trip points in consultation with the DNSP.

faqs.q: What should I do in an emergency?
faqs.a: Flip the AC isolator to OFF. This disconnects solar from your switchboard. Never try to work on the DC side.

faqs.q: How much sun does Wollongong get?
faqs.a: Wollongong averages 4.6–4.9 peak sun hours per day — among the best on the NSW coast.

faqs.q: Is Wollongong a good place for solar?
faqs.a: Yes. Good sun hours, coastal breezes that cool panels, and strong STC rebates make it one of NSW's best solar regions.

faqs.q: What about Shellharbour?
faqs.a: Same weather as Wollongong. Shellharbour's newer suburbs are often excellent candidates for 10kW+ solar.

faqs.q: Is Kiama good for solar?
faqs.a: Yes. Coastal winds keep panels cool and productivity high. Heritage restrictions apply in a few pockets.

faqs.q: What about Shoalhaven?
faqs.a: Excellent conditions — similar sun hours to Wollongong. Essential Energy (not Endeavour) handles grid connection in Nowra, Berry, etc.

faqs.q: Is Berry a good solar area?
faqs.a: Yes, with large blocks that support 10–13kW systems easily. Heritage considerations apply to Strand Street and surrounds.

faqs.q: What about Port Kembla and industrial areas?
faqs.a: Solar works well but some installs require extra attention due to dust and coastal salt spray.

faqs.q: How do bushfire zones affect solar?
faqs.a: BAL ratings above 29 (FZ, BAL-40) require specific materials and mounting. Your installer handles compliance.

faqs.q: Does salt spray damage panels?
faqs.a: Tier 1 panels are certified for coastal environments. Aluminium frames and tempered glass resist salt corrosion for 25+ years.

faqs.q: Who is the DNSP in my suburb?
faqs.a: Endeavour Energy covers Wollongong, Shellharbour, Kiama, Wollondilly. Essential Energy covers most of the Shoalhaven and south coast.

faqs.q: Are there Wollongong-specific rebates?
faqs.a: No council rebate. You access the federal STC, Cheaper Home Batteries Program, and retailer incentives like everyone else in NSW.

faqs.q: Why is the Illawarra good for solar?
faqs.a: Strong sun, coastal cooling, and a good mix of suburban homes with north-facing roofs make the Illawarra one of NSW's top solar regions.

faqs.q: What about winter performance?
faqs.a: Still strong. Illawarra winters are mild; a 6.6kW system produces 18–22 kWh/day even in July.

faqs.q: Is Coastal Solar Co. local?
faqs.a: Yes — we're based in Wollongong, CEC-accredited, and we install across the Illawarra and Shoalhaven.

faqs.q: How quickly can you install my system?
faqs.a: Most Illawarra installs happen within 3–6 weeks from signed contract to grid connection.

faqs.q: Are solar panels safe?
faqs.a: Yes. Properly installed panels comply with AS/NZS 5033 and pose minimal risk. Major risks are electrical (DC arcing), addressed by isolators and safety standards.

faqs.q: Can panels catch fire?
faqs.a: Very rarely. Fires are usually caused by poor wiring or DC isolator failures — not the panels themselves. Quality installs virtually eliminate this risk.

faqs.q: What is a DC isolator and why do I need it?
faqs.a: A DC isolator disconnects panels from the inverter. It's mandatory in Australia and critical for emergency response and maintenance safety.

faqs.q: Are solar batteries a fire risk?
faqs.a: Modern LFP batteries are chemically stable and fire-safe. Installation to AS/NZS 5139 further minimises any residual risk.

faqs.q: Can firefighters work on a house with solar?
faqs.a: Yes. Firefighters are trained for solar homes. Clearly-labelled isolators and compliant installs make their job safer.

faqs.q: What happens to solar during a blackout?
faqs.a: Grid-tied systems shut down for safety. Battery systems with backup mode continue supplying essential circuits.

faqs.q: Is rooftop solar safe for my roof structure?
faqs.a: Yes. Panels add ~12–15 kg/m² — well within Australian roof load ratings.

faqs.q: Do I need surge protection?
faqs.a: Most inverters include it. Homes in high-lightning areas may benefit from additional DC surge protection ($150–$400).

faqs.q: What should I do if I smell burning near the inverter?
faqs.a: Immediately flip the AC isolator off and call your installer or a licensed electrician. Don't open the inverter.

faqs.q: Are there EMF concerns with solar?
faqs.a: Solar panels and inverters produce extremely low EMF — far below WHO safety limits. No known health risk.
